## Sicheng Xue

### Part II (SQS \+ Workers):

Measured throughput while scaling worker counts processing orders from SQS.  
	•	1 worker → \~0.33 orders/sec  
	•	5 workers → \~1.67 orders/sec  
	•	20 workers → \~6.67 orders/sec  
	•	100 workers → \~33 orders/sec

Throughput increased nearly linearly with the number of workers.

### Part III (SNS → Lambda):

Replaced SQS workers with a Lambda function triggered directly by SNS.

CloudWatch logs show:  
	•	Cold start initialization ≈ 70–74 ms  
	•	Execution duration ≈ 3000 ms  
	•	Cold start overhead ≈ 2–3%

Cost:  
ECS workers ≈ $17/month vs Lambda ≈ $0/month (within free tier for \~10k orders).

Lambda removes operational overhead while maintaining acceptable performance.

## Yun Li

### Part II (SQS \+ Workers):

Measured queue depth and drain time while scaling worker counts processing orders from SQS.

1 worker → peak queue \~6.5K–8.5K, did not drain within 15+ min (CPU \~0%, Mem \~1.2%)  
5 workers → peak queue \~8.46K, did not drain within 1+ hour (CPU \~0%, Mem \~1.5%)  
20 workers → peak queue \~5.5K, drained to \~1.1K in \~1 h, did not reach 0 (CPU \~0%, Mem \~1.7%)  
100 workers → peak queue \~11.4K, did not drain—backlog from prior runs (CPU \~0–0.5%, Mem \~1.4%)  
With 20 workers the queue clearly started draining; at least \~20 workers are needed to avoid sustained buildup at \~60 orders/sec.

### 

### Part III (SNS → Lambda):

Replaced SQS workers with a Lambda function triggered directly by SNS.

CloudWatch logs show:

Cold start initialization ≈ 71–72 ms  
Execution duration ≈ 3000 ms  
Cold start overhead ≈ 2.4%

Cost:  
ECS workers ≈ $17/month vs Lambda ≈ $0/month (within free tier for \~10k orders).

Lambda removes the need to operate ECS workers and SQS; cost is $0 within the free tier and cold start overhead is negligible for 3 s processing.

## Siwen Wu![][image1]

## ![][image2]

## YumengZeng

### Part II (SQS \+ Workers):     

  Measured queue depth and drain time while scaling worker goroutines processing orders from SQS (\~57 orders/sec flash sale  
  load).                                                                                                                          
     
  Sync vs Async comparison:                                                                                                       
      • Sync (20 users): 95 orders in 60s, avg response 10,746ms (median 12s) — bottleneck                                      
      • Async (20 users): 1,550 orders in 60s, avg response 53ms — 16x more orders, 202x faster                               

  Worker scaling results:  
      • 1 worker  → \~0.33 orders/sec, peak queue \~5,225, drain time \~261 min (CPU \~0%, Mem \~1.5%)  
      • 5 workers → \~1.67 orders/sec, peak queue \~10,204, drain time \~102 min (CPU \~0%, Mem \~1.6%)  
      • 20 workers → \~6.67 orders/sec, peak queue \~12,728, drain time \~31 min (CPU \~0%, Mem \~1.7%)  
      • 100 workers → \~33 orders/sec, peak queue \~7,523, drained to \~161 in \~2 min (CPU \~12.7%, Mem \~1.7%)

  Throughput scales linearly with workers. Minimum \~171 workers needed to fully keep up with 57 orders/sec.  
  At 100 workers the CPU only hit 12.7% — bottleneck is I/O (network latency), not compute.

###  Part III (SNS → Lambda):

  Replaced SQS workers with a Lambda function triggered directly by SNS.

  CloudWatch logs show:  
      • Cold start initialization ≈ 77 ms  
      • Execution duration ≈ 3,002 ms  
      • Cold start overhead ≈ 2.6%  
      • Memory used: 20 MB / 512 MB allocated

  Cost:  
  ECS workers ≈ $17/month vs Lambda ≈ $0/month (within free tier for \~267K orders/month).

  Lambda removes all operational overhead (no queue monitoring, no worker scaling, no ECS health checks)  
  while maintaining acceptable performance. Cold start is negligible at 2.6% on 3s processing.  
  Switch to Lambda recommended for startups under 267K orders/month.
